title: Trailing Lambdas
description: The secret behind Kotlin's beautiful syntax.

slides.title: A Syntax Problem 🤨
slides.content:
## Parentheses Overload

When we passed our lambda into the `runTwice` function in the last lesson, it looked like this:
`runTwice( { println("Boom!") } )`

Notice how the lambda `{}` is trapped inside the parentheses `()`? As lambdas get longer (multiple lines of code), this becomes incredibly ugly and hard to read.

slides.title: The Trailing Convention 🛤️
slides.content:
Kotlin has a special rule: **If a lambda is the VERY LAST parameter a function accepts, you can move the lambda completely OUTSIDE the parentheses!**

This is called a **Trailing Lambda**.

quizData.question: What is the primary benefit of Trailing Lambdas?

options.text: They dramatically improve syntax readability, making the injected logic look like a native block.

quizData.explanation: Trailing lambdas are pure "syntactic sugar". They don't change how the system runs; they just make the code incredibly pleasant to read.

slides.title: Dropping the Parentheses 🚮
slides.content:
## Even Cleaner!

If the Higher-Order function legitimately ONLY takes a Lambda, and requires no other parameters (like numbers or strings), Kotlin lets you completely delete the empty `()` parentheses altogether!

quizData.question: If `fun repeat(times: Int, block: () -> Unit)` takes a Number AND a Lambda, which call syntax is correct?
quizData.explanation: Because the Lambda is the LAST parameter, it sits outside! The `(5)` represents the Int parameter, and the `{}` represents the Lambda parameter.
